[IMAGE:0]Yes, I'm back and have no disruptive plans for tomorrow. Midnight is almost upon us and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after that point will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 341,928th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.3 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week.  This is my seventy-third published book, and the result of an intellectual adventure that began forty-odd years ago when I first encountered a copy of that odd classic of American occultism, Peter Kelder's The Eye of Revelation.  It taught a set of allegedly Tibetan exercises called the Five Rites. They weren't Tibetan, but it took me a long strange trip to find out exactly where they came from. In the process, I got to learn much more than I expected about the history of American occultism and the stranger corners of bodywork...and all of that found its way into this book of mine. Interested?  You can get a copy here if you live in the US and here elsewhere.   

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

comments.content:
Hello JMG, got two today.

The first is, I've been thinking about some of your comments about enlightenment and what is an adept. I think I may have misunderstood what an "adept" is. Is an "adept" more like the mysterious figure who Paul Sedir met and travelled with? Or the 200 year old yogi that Robert Svoboda recounts meeting on many occasions? So adeptship and enlightenment are totally different things... in a comment recently (which I have been unable to find) you said something about how enlightenment is like a big orgasm? Do you think that's why celibacy is so often encouraged as a strategy towards it? Like all that energy is saved and then blown on a big experience? The thing is, the people who I know who have had that experience say the experience isn't the main thing - the main thing is the realization of the source of awareness.


Second - I've been working on a ritual modelled somewhat after the SOP for a little bit and I thought I'd see if you have any thoughts on it. So far I like it, but I'm still figuring it out. It's using the Kalachakra and the Digchakra directions and correspondances. Basically, as I was learning about these different chakras, I realized the Kala chakra basically banishes planetary energy, and the dig chakra invokes them, so... Here's a cool graphic from my Jyotish guru that overlays both chakras. https://shrifreedom.org/wp-content/uploads/VastuDigbalaPNG-300x216.png He encouraged me to experiment.

Here's the ritual:

Stand in the center and imagine a beam of light from the infinite above come down to the top of your head as you put your hands together at your crown while chanting a long om, bringing your hands down slowly to your chest. At your chest, pause for a second and say tat, then bring your hands out to your sides with the palms raised up and say sat, then bring them back together at your chest and say harih om


Go to the Eastern corner and/or face East.

Facing East intone om indraaya namah while tracing the Star of Lakshmi from the top point clockwise in bright white light as two squares
Say: “In the name and Vajra of Indra, the Eagle of the East, and the Sun/Surya, I invoke all the blessings of the Sun, including self-confidence, energy, vitality, clarity, and inner authority.”

Imagine the Sun shining on you in the morning, giving you all of it’s blessings while an eagle flies in the sky and you chant om namah suryaaya
Pause, and then say: “I thank the Sun/Surya for his gifts”

Then, still facing East, intone om indraaya namah while tracing the Star of Lakshmi from the top point, now counter-clockwise, in bright white light then say:
“in the name and Vajra of Indra, I banish arrogance, haughtiness, pride, self-centeredness and insecurity from me”

Imagine the Sun burning away all of your arrogance, haughtiness, pride and self-centeredness and leaving you pure then point to the middle of the Star and trace an arc in bright white light to the SE direction

Facing SE intone om agnaye namah while tracing the Star of Lakshmi from the top point clockwise in bright white light as two squares

Say: “In the name and Shakti of Agni, the cat of the SE, and the powers of Venus/Shukra, I invoke all of the blessings of Venus, including wealth, health/ojas, joy, beauty, love and devotion/bhakti”

Imagine Venus as a beautiful woman with a cat bestowing her blessings upon you while you chant om namah shukraaya
Pause, then say “I thank Venus for her gifts”

Then, still facing SE, intone om agnaye namah again while tracing the Star of Lakshmi in bright white light from the top point, this time count-clockwise
Say: “in the name and Shakti of Agni, I banish all anger, recklessness, accidents, violent thoughts and negative aspects of Mars from me” imagine fire burning away all of these negative things, then point to the middle of the Star and trace an arc in bright white light to the S direction


Facing South, intone om yamaaya namah while tracing the Star of Lakshmi as before, clockwise
Say: “in the name and Danda of Yama, the Lion of the South, and the powers of Mars/Mangala, I invoke all of the blessings of Mars, including discipline, courage, Will, decisiveness, and strength.”

Imagine Mangala as a fierce warrior with a lion beside him offering you his blessings, as you chant om namah mangalaaya
Pause, then say “I thank Mars for his gifts.”

Then, still facing South, again recite om yamaaya namah while tracing the Star in the same way, counter-clockwise.
Then say: “ In the name and Danda of Yama and the Lion of the South, I banish all gluttony, laziness, self-indulgence, greed and all negative qualities of Jupiter/Guru from me” as you imagine the lord of Death removing these negative things from you.
Then point to the middle of the Star and trace an arc in bright white light to the SW direction

Facing SW, intone om raakshaya namah while tracing the Star as before
Say: “In the name and Khadag of the Raaksha/Nrrti, the Dog of the SW, and the powers of Rahu, I invoke the blessings of Rahu, including innovative thinking, boons from foreigners, and material success”

Imagine the head of a Naga with a dog beside him bestowing his blessings on you as you chant om namah rahave
Pause, then say “I thank Rahu for his gifts.”

Then, still facing SW, intone om raakshaya namah/nirritaye whil tracing the Star counter-clockwise in the same way.
Then, say “In the name and Khadag of RaakshaNrrti, and the Dog of the SW, I banish all miscommunication, dishonesty and restlessness from me” and imagine these negative qualities leaving you.

Then point to the middle of the Star and trace an arc in bright white light to the W direction

Facing West, intone om varunaaya namah while tracing the Star as before, clockwise
Say: “In the name and Pasha of Varuna, the Snake of the West and the powers of Shani/Saturn, I invoke all of the blessings of Shani/Saturn, including endurance, longevity, long term thinking, perseverance, reliability, and wisdom.”

Imagine Shani/Saturn as an old, thin man with a limp leg, with a snake beside him, offering you his blessings as you recite om namah shanaishcaraaya
Pause, then say “I thank Shani for his gifts.”

Then, still facing West, intone om varunaaya namah again while tracing the Star counter-clockwise.

Then say: “In the name of Varuna and the Snake of the West, I banish all vanity, over-indulgence, lustful thoughts, jealousy and negative qualities of Venus/Shukra from me” as you imagine the waves of Varuna removing these negative qualities from you.
Then point to the middle of the Star and trace an arc in bright white light to the NW direction

Facing NW, intone om vaayave namah / om maarutaya namah?? While tracing the Star as before, clockwise.
Say: “In the name and Ankush of Vayu/Maaruta, the Mouse of the Northwest, and the powers of the Moon/Chandra, I invoke all of the blessings of the Moon, including emotional stability, financial prosperity, nourishment, mental peace, and harmonious relationships.”

Imagine the full Moon in the night, with a cute mouse basking in it, showering you with all the blessings of the Moon as you chant om namah chandraaya
Pause, then say “I thank the Moon for her gifts.”

Then, still in the NW, intone om vaayave namah again while tracing the Star as before, counter-clockwise.
Say: “In the name of Vayu/Maaruta and the Mouse of the Northwest, I banish all illness, fear, poverty, depressive thoughts and all negative qualities of Saturn from me” as you imagine the wind blowing away all of these negative things.
Then point to the middle of the Star and trace an arc in bright white light to the North

Facing North, intone om kuberaaya namah while tracing the Star as before, clockwise

Say: in the name and Gada of Kubera, the Elephant of the North and the powers of Budha/Mercury, I invoke all of the blessings of Budha/Mercury, including quick wit, eloquence, business success, and clarity in communication.”

Imagine Budha/Mercury as a youthful god in green clothes with an elephant beside him granting you his blessings as you chant om namah budhaaya
Pause, then say “I thank Budha/Mercury for his gifts.”

Then, still facing North, intone om kuberaaya namah again while tracing the Star counter-clockwise
Say: “In the name of Kubera and the Elephant of the North, I banish all fickleness, emotional insecurity, moodiness and negative qualities of the Moon from me” and imagine all of these negative things dissolving.
Then point to the middle of the Star and trace an arc in bright white light to the NE

In the NE, intone om iishaanaaya namah while tracing the Star as before, clockwise
Say: “In the name of Ishana, the Hare of the Northeast, and Jupiter/Guru, I invoke all of the blessings of Jupiter/Guru, including wealth, luck, good decisions, optimism, generosity, and higher knowledge”

Imagine Jupiter as a brahmin in yellow, wearing a sacred thread, with a mala and a stick, bestowing his blessings upon you as you chant om gurave namah
Pause, then say “I thank Guru for his gifts.”

Still in the NE, again intone om iishaanaya namah while tracing the Star counter-clockwise
Say: “In the name and Shoola of Ishaana, and the Hare of the Northeast, I banish all confusion, anxiety, addictive tendencies, deception and the negative qualities of Rahu from me.” Imagine these leaving you.
Then point to the middle of the Star and trace an arc in bright white light to the East.

In the East again, come back to the center.

Feel your body sinking with your breath into the earth and say om padamajaaya namah
“I ask for the blessings of the Lotus Born.” Imagine the blessings enter you.

Feel your consciousness rising with the breath and chant om anantaaya namah, then say:
“I ask for the blessings of the Celestial Serpent” and imagine the blessings enter you.

Cycle the breath down as you feel your materiality with the outbreath, then back up to awareness of your limitless consciousness with the in breath. Do this for a while, then imagine a beam of light from the infinite above come down to the top of your head as you put your hands together at your crown while chanting a long om, bringing your hands down slowly to your chest. At your chest, pause for a second and say tat, then bring your hands out to your sides with the palms raised up and say sat, then bring them back together at your chest and say harih om

replies.title: Re: 2 Questions
replies.content:
1) It's a little simpler than that. The experience that gets called "enlightenment" in English resembles orgasm, not as an expression of sexual energy, but simply in that it's a very intense, essentially indescribable experience that takes a relatively short time and then is over. The mere fact that you have had it doesn't guarantee future wisdom or holiness; in fact, there's a Zen saying that one who achieves instantaneous complete enlightenment is like an arrow shot straight into Hell. (Keeping the ego under control in that situation, once the rush of the experience fades, is not easy at all.) Adeptship, by contrast, represents a condition of mastery earned, not by a single spasmodic event, but by long years of hard work culminating in a steady state of high spiritual attainment. The great teachers of India qualify, for example; Mouni Sadhu, who knew as much as anyone in his generation about Western esotericism, is on record as saying that Sri Ramana Maharshi was a genuine adept.

2) I don't have enough of a background in Hinduism to be able to judge your symbolism. As for the ritual, it seems a little long and ornate from my perspective, but if that's what appeals to you, give it a try and see what results you get.

replies.title: Re: 2 Questions
replies.content:
Ok, thanks. That makes sense. For what it's worth, I know people who have had relatively "random" enlightenment experiences, but most of the people I know have put in many years of hard work in the direction of self knowledge.

And thanks for the comments on the ritual - yes it is a little ornate, and perhaps I can simplify it a bit, but it's not longer than an LBRP/MP/ALU sequence. Half the reason to do it is to internalize this knowledge while deepening my studies into Jyotish.

I would be interested if other members of the commentariat with more of a background in esoteric Hinduism have any thoughts, and of course there's an open invitation for others to try this ritual if it appeals, and let me know your experiences.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Well, okay. (I don't really know anywhere that I could go to start with doing that, because it's so uncommon for anyone to run their forums in such a way as to permit questions that aren't visibly associated with consistent pseudonym handles, and that greatly decreases the mixing of the "anonymity pool". Almost no other forum participants in that case are going to also be doing anything like choosing one-off handles from an uninformative sequence of handles by default, and that's assuming that it's even a forum platform that makes it possible to sign up multiple times at all.)

In case anyone else might have experienced a situation like this: The context is that, across some 12 years' worth of good-faith attempted participation in Catholic rites, I never experienced any sense of meaning or presence in any of the main-line liturgy or observances -- except for once, when I was at a Holy Saturday adoration. (Similarly, Bible study never did anything for me either, except for a random extended emotional response to the reference to "treasure in earthen vessels" in 2 Cor 4:7, accompanied by an unexplained impulse to pay sustained attention to it. But I assumed that this was just what Ignatius calls a false consolation -- the loose equivalent of the Zen concept of makyō -- because, at the time, it didn't recognizably connect with anything else or go anywhere.)

Now, there's a confounding potential explanation for that sense of presence or meaning: the only other people present at the Holy Saturday adoration were like fifteen of the parish's older serious devotional practitioners, and it was a small side chapel space. But the potential explanation there doesn't fit very well with another observation, which is that none of the weekday Masses did anything for me either, even though you'd think they'd only draw relatively devoted people also. And also, on the other side of the scale, I've got a small mountain of side circumstantial evidence suggesting that I either have some kind of strong chthonic or telluric affinity, or at least that I have some strong investment from some "outside" such source / archetype, that I've just poorly individuated from / interrogated and reintegrated / whatever it is that one is supposed to do with such things.

And (for complicated other reasons) I worry that maybe I will turn out to have objectively had some kind of duty to try harder and debug what might have been going on there.

(Okay, I know that an important part of Axial religious egregors in practice, especially Abrahamic ones, is a sort of internalized "moral-steadfastness self-gaslighting" towards anyone who is lukewarm or noncompliant or dubious in ways that threaten to prove inconvenient for the propagation of whichever system of religious beliefs it is. But still. What if it really had in some sense been nothing but my fault that almost nothing worked for me?

But maybe that doesn't make sense. Orthodox Christianity is like "if it doesn't work for you, then that's because you don't have the right childlike acceptance or the right humility or something". And I guess heterodox Christianity is sometimes more like "if it doesn't work for you, then maybe that's because you don't have the right gnosis yet". But this seems more like, "if it doesn't work for you, then that's because you just didn't try hard enough to guess which unlikely and probably-heretical books you were supposed to have scoured instead, and so you lacked the right random technical insight about individual predispositions associated with obscure cosmological distinctions like 'chthonic' vs. 'celestial' that only like 0.1% of the faithful have even heard of and only like 2% even of trained exorcists are aware might have relevant forces associated with them". And that really sounds kind of discordant with the Christian narrative about how access to grace by effectively-helpless metaphorical sheep is supposed to work. If standard official catechesis is defective in respect to crucially relevant information that chthonic-aligned metaphorical helpless sheep need to hear, and it's information that anyone with the relevant supernatural perceptual faculties ought trivially to have been able to gather...

Well. It certainly wouldn't be surprising to find yet another place where Jn 14:12 ("the works that I do shall he do also; and greater works than these shall he do") were going unfulfilled. But then, I suppose the most obvious Christian line of counterargument here would instead just be something like, "the only thing that anyone with teaching authority should have promised you was that the grace would be accessible, not that it would be at all perceivable -- that is, that you would ever experience anything other than meaningless-feeling aridity." Which is true. Nobody with official teaching authority ever did promise me that.)

I only made this connection about Holy Saturday and chthonicness like a couple weeks ago, and so this is me in the middle of trying to work out what practical implications there might be.

Holy Saturday is coming up this weekend in the Western rite, if there are any other Catholic liturgy non-responders out there who want to test this variable! ...Except maybe you should only attend the service, if it would be unusual to show up at the adoration. And then I'm not sure the effect would be strong enough in that case.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
In a previous Magic Monday, I mentioned learning Japanese, and have two questions on one aspect of it I'm struggling with that I've realized has an occult dimension as well as a linguistic one. There are a lot of metaphors embedded into any language, and Japanese has quite a few that are extremely alien to the ones in English. For instance, the past is quite often expressed with terms for "ahead" and the future is expressed with terms for "behind" in Japanese; the exact opposite of English.

In cases where English lacks similar metaphors, I have no problem learning the Japanese ones. For instance, "hand-neck" and "foot-neck" as terms for wrist and ankle respectively are not causing me problems. In cases where the English metaphorical system is different, however, I am having a hard time shedding the English metaphorical interpretations.

a) Aside from trying to think through why the metaphor makes sense and sticking with it until the new metaphorical system sinks in, do you have advice for learning how to let go of one metaphorical system and embracing the other?

b) I've noticed a lot of older occult systems use radically different kinds of symbolism. In the Hermetic Golden Dawn, for instance, there are the elements, the planets, and the spheres, and the symbolisms assigned to these are quite often radically different in key points. I haven't done a lot of practical rituals yet, and so this has mostly been an academic question for me, but I am planning more serious practical magic in the future, and one of the things I've realized is that each of these symbolic frameworks is better suited for some purposes compared with others. A system of magic with only one symbolic framework is in some senses limited by its symbolic framework; just like certain aspects of how time works are harder to think about and discuss in English, certain ritual purposes are harder to reach with any given symbolic framework.

However, just like a large number of English speakers are monolingual, a lot of the modern magical systems in the English speaking world are mono-symbolic. Do you think the loss of this sort of multiple symbolic systems coexisting in one system of magic might be related to the loss of bilingualism in much of the English speaking world?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Use a ritual to shift from one to the other. For example, you can get a ring or something else, which you put on only when you're studying or speaking Japanese. Make it a regular habit, and state-dependent memory will make it easier for you to remember Japanese metaphors when you wear the ring.

2) Yes, and both are functions of the pervasive embrace of intellectual monocultures in modern Western society. It's what Blake called "single vision":

"Now I a fourfold vision see
And a fourfold vision is given to me
Tis fourfold in my supreme delight
And three fold in soft Beulahs night
And twofold Always. May God us keep
From Single vision & Newtons sleep"

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
This is not occult related but the Japanese metaphors 前 and 後ろ are actually the same as in English.

前 = before, as in "who stands before me?"

後ろ is simply behind.

In Latin and the Romance languages, "ante" or before is also used in this sense.

Useful note: from Accra, Ghana (in the heart of the old "slave coast," where most of the African slaves in the Caribbean region came from) to Cabinda, Angola (near the mouth of the Congo river, where most of the African slaves in the US came from) is roughly the same distance as from New York City to Boise, Idaho. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You'd think so, right? The fact of the matter, though, is that nobody knows where the word "hoodoo" comes from, and the two traditions have essentially nothing in common. Hoodoo traces its roots to Bakongo spirituality, which is a rich and thoroughly worked out animism in which nkisi, spiritual power, is found in minkisi, objects of power. You can find a decent summary of the tradition here:

https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kongo_religion

Interestingly, the core emblem of Bakongo religion is a cross in a circle -- what gets called a "Celtic cross" in many other contexts. Its teachings resonate closely enough with Druidry that I've occasionally wondered about the possibility of influence; the hypothesis of a drowned civilization in the Atlantic basin during the late Ice Age, i.e., "Atlantis," comes to mind as a common source.

I don't know if you refer to something similar but in my own specific tradition of syncretic folk mexican magic that term is used, but as most folk terms, it doesn't mean the same thing as the academic sense.

In this tradition the head houses a part of "the soul" that handles daily things and vital energies given at birth, we call it tonal (toh-NAHL) --the astral body for our intents and purposes.

There are two diagnoses used for the maladies of this: susto: which means freight. Part of you is left at the spot where the thing that scared you (and you felt your soul leave your body a little bit, so to speak) happened. Say, you tripped and fell down, and got shaken. comments.title: The Mystical Relationship
comments.content:
Greetings, JMG and all!

In a recent comment you mentioned that, in a nutshell, magic pursues or relates to power, mysticism to love, and occultism to wisdom. I'm sure this is true in a great many instances, but I'd like to go deeper into the mysticism equation. As practiced in Christianity, in bhakti yoga, among bodhisattvas, and so forth, mysticism as love certainly is true. Perhaps in Islamic Sufism, too, though what little I've learned about that seems more like 'delight or joy' but love and delight are of course closely intertwined.

But I suggest that what really happens in the mystical relationship depends on the nature of the Deity one is relating to. When the Deity is focused on love then the mystical experience is love, including various different forms of love in pagan and tribal religions as well as Christian agape. For example, the mystics devoted to nymphs in ancient Greek and Roman practice. Those can be contrasted to the Bacchae and Maenads, whose mystical experiences were not anything like what we would understand as love, yet I think it's true that they were genuine mystical experiences, and were cultivated and sought-for as mystics do. Though one might argue for a differentiation between 'ecstatic states' and 'mysticism,' I suppose.

For myself, I would say that my mystical relationships and experiences are more focused on insight and understanding: I am fascinated to learn and experience how the Deities themselves view the Worlds, life, humans, nature, each other, phenomena, events, cosmology and cosmogony--everything. The 'oneness' that I experience with them isn't focused on the relationship itself, but on perceptually entering into the Deity's experiences and worldview. Obviously, my ability to do so is limited by my individual humanness, but I don't let that stop me from pursuing it as well as I can. The necessarily incomplete nature of my understanding is something both I and the Deities can simply accept and work with, and they seem to enjoy the work as much as I do.

And needless to say, their viewpoints, understandings, and experiences vary considerably from Deity to Deity, so there is a great deal of variety in my mystical experiences and my efforts at understanding. Love is not absent, but it's not the main focus, either--love is more of an emergent property arising from the relationships and experiences, that happens while other stuff is going on. And it's the 'other stuff,' the seeking of knowledge, understanding, and experience, that draws me and motivates me to pursue these relationships.

I think that the way I pursue polytheistic, Heathen mysticism is more focused on wisdom than on love, but it does produce love and is nourished by it. This wisdom aspect is why I find your writing, insights, and wisdom so fascinating, while still needing to pursue my own way of seeking it and constellating it into my worldview and practice.

I would enjoy reading more about your and others' views and experiences concerning the pursuit of mysticism!

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: The Mystical Relationship
replies.content: Oh, granted. I was speaking specifically to the mystical traditions that have been most common in the Western world, and primarily to Christian and Jewish mysticism. Outside that framework mysticism is a much broader thing.

replies.title: Re: The Mystical Relationship
replies.content:
Hi Winifred,
Don't have an answer to your question except tangentially - I've been thinking about metaphors of mysticism and occultism and the idea of trying to navigate the vagaries/stupidity of ego by introducing a focus on the higher self into the process.
Came across this from Inayat Khan:
"People often ask what is the difference between mysticism and occultism. In point of fact occultism is that which the mystic shows as the result of his experience, as the outcome of his insight, as the expression of divine law. Nowadays we often hear occultism spoken of as something distinct from mysticism, but that is not so."
http://www.hazrat-inayat-khan.org/php/views.php?h1=39&h2=1&h3=0&p=15

And am doing an experiment to see if it is possible to use the mystic’s search for the divine as part of practical work towards developing the ‘mental body’.

Superficially similar to the Taoist and Dolmen Arch metaphors of lower, middle and upper cauldrons, but different in that this is an experiment to see if it is possible to bridge the apparent ‘gap’ in the methods of practical occult philosophy and mystic philosophy; that is, to make use of what Brunton calls the quest for the overself and the Sufis call ‘the beloved’ (the search for the higher self / soul to allow the higher self to manifest itself as much as it can in the limited physical form) in conjunction with deliberate inner development methods in style of Dolmen Arch and Taoist practices.

Explained a different way – it is an experiment to try and introduce the capacities of the higher self into the metaphor process of developing ‘wisdom’ of the mental body or light body – this is not about the Taoist idea of becoming a physical immortal, it is about creating a situation where occult development is not driven just by the ego personality, but to introduce a tuning towards the true self that is already [effectively] immortal so that time here in this realm is most effective / useful to the soul and ‘more’ useful to the world.

And another from Inayat Khan:

“Sympathy is the main quality to be cultivated in order to develop the spiritual faculty; but if one would ask me what I mean by sympathy, it is something I cannot explain. All such words are different names, different aspects of one and the same thing. What is called sympathy, kindness, mercy, goodness, pity, compassion, gentleness, humility, appreciation, gratefulness, service, is in reality love. And what is love? Love is God.”

Where Khan refers to God I get the impression he is referring to the Absolute (e.g Tao) but not as abstract, but relationship with a being... he said something along the lines of 'If you don't have a deity/Ideal, invent one".

Deities embodying/expressing particular Ideals is interesting to consider.

replies.title: Re: The Mystical Relationship
replies.content:
This is a very interesting and challenging endeavor you are working on, Earthworm! Thank you for the good explanation you give. I wish you all success with it!

It is, however, quite different from the experiences I was writing about, not that this makes either way 'wrong' or mistaken, of course. Just different paths. The path you write about is focused on the Self, the Self of a human person. The path of many forms of mysticism is focused on the "I-Thou" relationship between a human self and the chosen, or choosing, Deity, as Martin Buber so beautifully wrote about.

The path I'm on is focused on the Deities themselves. Obviously, my own Self is involved here, but rather than focusing on the relationship between us, I want to get to know the Gods themselves as well as I am able. For example, how does Heimdall view this world of ours and all its events and phenomena, with his penetrating sight and hearing? What does he think and feel about what he observes? How is he immanent in this world? What does he want to see our world becoming? How about other Worlds? Where does he travel, what does he do, how does he interact with other Deities and spirits? What does he know of past and future? What insights and wisdom does he have to share on Heathen mysteries and life in general? What will he tell me of his own story? And the same for all the rest of the Deities in my pantheon.

I don't mean this in any snoopy sense; the Deities have no trouble keeping their own privacy as they wish! Nor do I imagine that what I perceive in these endeavors is the whole or only 'truth' about them, nor would I try to misuse or abuse anything I thought I learned from them. But pursuing this path expands my horizons, grows my heart and mind and all of my Self, opens new doors, sparks new ideas, inspires me to share and learn from others too, and is deeply satisfying. It works so well, too, with the system of Heathen soul lore that I believe they helped me develop--and again, I make no claims that any of this is the 'one and only way' to do anything! It's just how I do it.

But I do really enjoy learning about other ways, others' experiences, and seeing how much it all fits together and shines light on the mysteries in interesting ways. Thank you for telling me about yours!

Winifred

replies.title: Re: The Mystical Relationship
replies.content:
Hello Winifred and all
I always liked how Dion Fortune contrasted mysticism to magic. From my understanding, she said in mysticism absolutely nothing stands between seeker and the Divine… body, mind, sensory delights etc. are sacrificed on the altar of pure devotion, and, in fact, are considered impediments towards the goal of “union” with the Divine.
With magic, all faculties serve as functions to pursue a relationship with the Divine, hence her maxim that good work can only be done with good tools in such service.
Lately, I’ve come to define occultism (in my mind) simply as “mind training”, but with a sophisticated approach to both “mind” and various trainings.

Always lovely to hear from you Winifred and always pranams to JMG for hosting this forum of ideas.

2. Concerning the symbolism of the Rider-Waite deck and those in the same tradition: It's very interesting but having worked with them for some time now, I find that the meanings do not always map reliably to the traditional meanings given by, say, Mathers or Waite. I liked to use them as a guide in the early days, but their interpretations can't always be relied upon. Also, I think they tend to the most extreme interpretations of the symbolism, perhaps to make the symbolism very clear; although this can also be misleading in its own way.

To give a very specific example of what I mean, the suit of swords seems "off" in general. The interpretations given for it are often more malefic than what I find in practice. For me, swords often come up in queries where engagement with society is expected. For example, 9s and 10s of swords will come up when there's a busy or agitated social climate. They needn't mean desolation or disaster. However, sometimes they do still have their traditional meanings.

Sometimes the symbolism can be quite literal as well: One day the Tower came up in a 3-card daily reading and I ended up visiting a tower that day! Also, the Tower came up in the outcome card on a day when I was traveling, which made me uneasy, but what it seemed to mean was ambition and energy in this case.

Thought it might be interesting and helpful to point this out as many people have probably had similar experiences working with this and other oracles.

comments.title: Desperate friend in need
comments.content: Hello JMG. A friend told me that he is willing to accept any white-collar job with moderately decent pay. He's been laid off for a full year now. What kind of magick would you hypothetically if you were in this situation? It seems his situation needs a lot of magickal power.

replies.title: Re: Desperate friend in need
replies.content:
He's in a very difficult situation, because the supply of white-collar jobs with "moderately decent" (i.e., high) pay is contracting sharply these days. This is part of a major historical change and will not reverse in our lifetimes. In his place I'd cut my expectations sharply, learn to get by on much less money, and take a job that will keep me fed and housed for the time being, while developing new job skills -- preferably aimed at self-employment rather than finding a position in a big corporation or bureaucracy.

There's a broader lesson here. You cannot use magic to make the universe reverse course; if winter's coming, no spell you are capable of casting will give you summer weather. What you can do is use magic to enable you to deal with winter more effectively. In the same way, if you live in a declining civilization and it's about to shed large sections of its managerial class, magic won't shield you from that -- though it can help you make the transition to the less white-collar-centric economy ahead.

comments.title: Q
comments.content:
Greetings JMG,

1) You mentioned this a few sessions ago, so could you share more about the saturn as the old sun story or myth (or how true it is)? I thought this was just some fancy tale.

2) what are archetypical images? and can origin stories of say Jesus be considered that, or is that more just a myth?

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
1) There's a tradition in some occult sources that Saturn is in some sense "the old Sun" -- that is, that it's what's left of the star at the center of a solar system older than ours. Whether that's symbolically or literally true, or a mix of both, is an interesting question I can't answer. It's not impossible, though, that the part of the primal dust cloud that became the planet Saturn contains some of the material from a long-destroyed star -- more of this than other parts of the solar system.

2) The archetypes, according to Jung, are the releasing images of the human instincts. You might have read that newly hatched goslings are preprogrammed from birth to recognize the largest moving object near them as Mom; the scientist Konrad Lorenz used to hatch goose eggs while sitting among them, and get himself adopted as Mom by a flock of baby geese. The "releasing image" is the hardwired pattern in the brain that Lorenz triggered. Humans have them too; we all have a Mom image, a Dad image, a Spouse image, an Enemy image, and so on. These are the archetypes: the Great Mother, the Great Father, the Anima or Animus (depending on gender), and the Shadow, respectively.

Myths have the emotional power they do because they work with those images. The story of the birth of Jesus, then, isn't an archetype, but it triggers the emotional energies of important archetypes and that's what gives it its emotional power. A myth can be true, by the way, or it can be fictional -- good fiction works with archetypes, too.

replies.title: Re: Q
replies.content:
Thanks!

On 2, a month or so ago, I'd asked you about the nature of this post I found on reddit about the alchemical marriage (union of opposites) and return to the one, and you had referred to it as an archetypical image.

It seems like you meant something else there, rather than the Jungian instinct-images.


https://www.reddit.com/r/awakened/comments/r7lzx4/on_the_alchemical_marriage_the_return_to_union/

"religious and esoteric traditions.

These traditions generally include the following elements:

the primordial angelic hermaphrodite

the notion of a divine soulmate or twin

the dual forces of mind and matter (these are usually assigned a gender, but not always)

the descent of the soul into matter for a purpose and the ascent back to unity, following a successful incarnation

the eternal nature of consciousness and the soul

an acknowledgment of the higher realms, i.e. the astral plane

Humans are composed of two opposites: mind - immortal Being; body - mortal Becoming. These opposites trouble us and produce a dynamical, life-long process of spiritual growth. In psychological understanding, this process is directed toward the realization of one's essential person, one's Platonic "higher self".

The periodic descent back into matter fuels novelty generation and the Individuation process. Your Self is eternal, and this realm is you fishing for more unique experiences that color your true divine identity.

Bringing forth the immortal person (known as Jungian individuation), living fulfilling life guided by divine providence, and reuniting with your twin soul in a hieros gamos, or holy marriage, is the most noble goal of many of these traditions. It is captured in the alchemists’ description of the Great Work or Magnum Opus of the "Rebis": married in the physical, United once again in spirit."

"Hermeticism, Jungian Psychology, and Myth

Hermes can be thought of as the God voice in bicameralism that reveals itself once the brain hemispheres have been balanced and synchronized. Hermes, the messenger of the gods or “god of travelers/boundaries”, literally means “heap of stones, boundary marker.” The duality from which this reference stems is life and death – the heap of stones at a gravesite that stands at the threshold between the material and spiritual. The word hermaphrodite, which shares the same root, is characterized by having attributes of both sexes – a literal manifestation of a higher level integration of the anima/animus.

Similarly, Shiva (Purusha) and counterpart Parvati/Kali/Shakti (Prakriti) were originally a merged entity known as Ardhanarishvara. When rejoined and reconcile, they reside back in the heart center.

The spiritual journey (i.e. one's awakening) is symbolically represented on Hermes’ staff, the caduceus – the two snakes of the kundalini (representing the male and female -Shakti- energies of earth) wind their way up the spine, activating each chakra, until they ultimately bootstrap the soul and set it free into the heavens (represented by the wings at the top of the staff).

Spiritual liberation can thus be seen as a four step, sequential, duality-bridging process. The female aspect first liberates the male spiritually (integration of the anima), the male liberates the female materially (the slaying of the dragon), the female liberates the male materially (the holy marriage), the male liberates the female spiritually (the integration of the animus) – this final step is hinted at by Andromeda’s very name, which means “to be mindful of man.” In Jungian terms, the result is a quaternio hieros gamos – a holy marriage between the four aspects of the two individuals.

These phenomenon are also highly related to the myth of Cupid and Psyche: the root of psychedelic is of course "psyche" and in Greek "Psyche'"means breath, life, soul. She was a woman in myth - actually, the twin soul of Cupid himself. Those wings pictured on Psyche and Cupid are astral wings, same as on the caduceus.

Hinduism also tells us about the androgynous creator. It is we creator because at the beginning all consciousness was in one and the same person also called Adam Kadmon in Kaballah for example. This is the primordial "I am" that is also called Eros, Phanes, Protogonos in Greek mythology

Ardhanarishvara represents the synthesis of masculine and feminine energies of the universe (Purusha and Prakriti) and illustrates how Shakti, the female principle of God, is inseparable from (or the same as, according to some interpretations) Shiva, the male principle of God. The union of these principles is exalted as the root and womb of all creation. The relevant image here is the Hindu Shatkona, which you may know as the Star of David or Merkaba: the union of the upward (Shiva, Purusha) and downward (Shakti, Prakriti) triangles. This is Adam and Eve, reunited again, reentering the garden."


there's more in that link but I'd thought I'd share these two.

comments.title: storms in the group mind
comments.content:
Greetings!

In Psychic Self -Defense, Dion Fortune describes something that is a sort of precursor to her Magical Battle of Britain work.


Some complications arose at one time over an Indian occultist who was visiting this country in order to found a school. He was deeply involved in the politics of his own country, and there could be no doubt about it that he disliked the English and all their ways very much indeed. I think that I was the only pure-blooded Anglo-Saxon who was in touch with him. As far as I know, he did not concern himself with mundane plane political activities, his idea being to organise a meditation group which should pour the
regenerative spiritual force of the East into the group- soul of the British Empire, which he
declared was in a very bad way indeed. I maintained, however, that the group-soul was not dying, as
he held, but very tired, for it was immediately after the War. Moreover, I could not see how any
body who disliked it so very much was going to be able to regenerate it. Nor was I sure that the
regeneration was going to be to our taste if we were to get it. This man, whom I will call X., was
of an intense spiritual pride, and his root-idea was that England must acknowledge the spiritual
supremacy of India and take her spiritual inspiration from the East. I was young and inexperienced
at the time, but I began to ask myself what manner of spiritual force was going to be poured in
through the channel we were constructing. Supposing during the War a group of English Occultists
had tried to perform a similar service for Germany, what line would they have taken? Would they not
have tried to influence the German group-mind to give up its militarist ideals and concentrate on
the League of Nations? Was it not more than likely that our Indian friend was trying to disabuse us
of our Imperialistic tendencies? Would it not appear to him, smarting as he was under the race
prejudice of the white man, that the world would be a much better place for humanity if the English
cultivated their own garden and let other people alone? I got more and more uneasy, and X., being a
good psychic, detected my uneasiness, and I was asked to withdraw from the
group he was organising.

I felt quite convinced that something sinister was being attempted against the group-mind of my
race, but I had no means of gauging its extent or potency. This was not the kind of tale one could
take to Scotland Yard; moreover, several of my personal friends believed in the bonafides of X.
and were taking part in the group he was organising, and I was very anxious not to involve them in any unpleasantness.


There's more to the story, but, in addition to her remarks about the impropriety of trying to influence directly the group-mind of another nation, there is also a great deal about what might be called the national psychic immune system of various nations.

In the light of these remarks, how might one characterize what has been happening to Britain over the last few decades?

LeGrand
